file_name: OTISWORLDWIDECORP_04_03_2020-EX-10.4-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT by and among UNITED TECHNOLOGIES CORPORATION, OTIS WORLDWIDE CORPORATION and CARRIER ~1.txt

perturbation.original_text: Subject to Section 3.2, each Assignor Party, on behalf of itself and the other members of the Assignor Group, hereby irrevocably assigns to the applicable Assignee Party, and agrees to irrevocably assign to the applicable Assignee Party, all of its and the other members of the Assignor Group's rights, title and interest in and to any and all Intellectual Property Rights owned by the Assignor Party or another member of the Assignor Group that meets one or more of the following descriptions: (a) the Intellectual Property Rights are Requester Foreground IPR conceived or created in the course of services concerning which the Assignee Party or another member of the Assignee Group was the Requester, and the Assignor Party or another member of the Assignor Group was the Performer; or (b) the Intellectual Property Rights are Performer Foreground-Delivered IPR conceived or created in the course of services concerning which the Assignee Party or another member of the Assignee Group was the Requester, and the Assignor Party or another member of the Assignor Group was the Performer; or
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to Section 3.2, each Assignor Party, on behalf of itself and the other members of the Assignor Group, will endeavor to assign to the applicable Assignee Party, and agrees to assign to the applicable Assignee Party to a reasonable extent, its and the other members of the Assignor Group's rights, title and interest in and to Intellectual Property Rights owned by the Assignor Party or another member of the Assignor Group that arguably meets one or more of the following descriptions: (a) the Intellectual Property Rights could be Requester Foreground IPR conceived or created in the course of services concerning which the Assignee Party or another member of the Assignee Group was the Requester, and the Assignor Party or another member of the Assignor Group was the Performer; or (b) the Intellectual Property Rights might be Performer Foreground-Delivered IPR conceived or created in the course of services concerning which the Assignee Party or another member of the Assignee Group was the Requester, and the Assignor Party or another member of the Assignor Group was the Performer; or
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the Assignor Party irrevocably assigns Intellectual Property Rights. The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'irrevocably assigns' to 'will endeavor to assign' and 'agrees to assign to a reasonable extent,' and by changing 'are' to 'could be' and 'might be,' making the assignment discretionary and uncertain. This weakens the obligation to assign IP rights, creating a potential loophole for non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding contract enforceability requires clear and definite terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 6, § 2-204 requires that a contract be sufficiently definite for a court to determine whether a breach has occurred and to provide an appropriate remedy. By making the assignment of IP rights dependent on 'endeavor to assign' and 'to a reasonable extent,' and by making the IP status unclear using 'could be' and 'might be,' the modified text introduces significant ambiguity, making it difficult to determine the exact scope of the obligation and whether it has been breached, which makes the provision potentially unenforceable under Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces significant ambiguity regarding the assignment of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR). By changing 'irrevocably assigns' to 'will endeavor to assign' and 'agrees to assign to a reasonable extent,' and by changing 'are' to 'could be' and 'might be,' the obligation becomes discretionary and uncertain, weakening the commitment to assign IP rights. According to the Law Explanation, this ambiguity could make it difficult for a court to determine if a breach has occurred, potentially rendering the provision unenforceable under Delaware law. While the provided scraped snippet focuses on sales under UCC Article 2, the Law Explanation explicitly references Delaware Code Title 6, § 2-204 concerning contract definiteness, which the modified language undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Otis and Carrier, on behalf of themselves and the other members of, respectively, the Otis Group and the Carrier Group, agree and promise to assist UTC and the other members of the UTC Group, at UTC's request, in UTC's discretion and at UTC's cost, in applying for, registering, maintaining, renewing, demonstrating use of, recording UTC's and the other members of the UTC Group's rights in, and otherwise perfecting, and defending and enforcing against third party infringers, the rights of UTC and the other members of the UTC Group in the United Technologies Trademarks and all goodwill associated therewith, including executing, verifying, acknowledging and delivering any and all documents, including any instruments of transfer and recordable assignments, and confirmations of use, and performing such other acts deemed necessary in the reasonable opinion of UTC.
perturbation.changed_text: Otis and Carrier, on behalf of themselves and the other members of, respectively, the Otis Group and the Carrier Group, shall consider assisting UTC and the other members of the UTC Group, at UTC's request, at a mutually agreeable cost, in potentially applying for, registering, maintaining, renewing, demonstrating use of, recording UTC's and the other members of the UTC Group's rights in, and generally perfecting, and defending against third party infringers when feasible, the rights of UTC and the other members of the UTC Group in the United Technologies Trademarks and associated goodwill, perhaps executing, verifying, acknowledging and delivering various documents and performing acts perceived as helpful.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates Otis and Carrier to assist UTC with trademark-related activities. The modified version weakens this obligation by replacing 'agree and promise to assist' with 'shall consider assisting,' 'UTC's discretion and at UTC's cost' with 'at a mutually agreeable cost,' 'applying for, registering, maintaining, renewing, demonstrating use of, recording UTC's and the other members of the UTC Group's rights in, and otherwise perfecting, and defending and enforcing against third party infringers' with 'potentially applying for, registering, maintaining, renewing, demonstrating use of, recording UTC's and the other members of the UTC Group's rights in, and generally perfecting, and defending against third party infringers when feasible,' and 'any and all documents, including any instruments of transfer and recordable assignments, and confirmations of use, and performing such other acts deemed necessary in the reasonable opinion of UTC' with 'various documents and performing acts perceived as helpful.' These changes make the assistance optional and subjective, diminishing the enforceable obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law requires that contractual obligations be stated with sufficient clarity.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware Law, Judson Roberts Co., Inc. v. Sevenson Envtl. Servs., Inc., 2007 WL 3232288, at *11 (Del. Ch. Oct. 26, 2007)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts have emphasized that contracts must articulate the obligations of the parties clearly. In Judson Roberts Co., Inc. v. Sevenson Envtl. Servs., Inc., the Delaware Chancery Court required that all contractual obligations between parties must be specified. The modified text's use of ambiguous language such as 'shall consider assisting,' 'mutually agreeable cost,' 'potentially applying for', 'when feasible,' and 'perceived as helpful' undermines the clarity of the obligation, rendering it vague and difficult to enforce under Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that directly contradicts the principle of clearly defined contractual obligations. The original text mandates assistance with trademark-related activities, while the changed text weakens this obligation using ambiguous language such as 'shall consider assisting,' 'mutually agreeable cost,' 'potentially applying for', 'when feasible,' and 'perceived as helpful.' The Law Explanation asserts that Delaware courts, as in Judson Roberts Co., Inc. v. Sevenson Envtl. Servs., Inc., require contractual obligations to be specified. By making the assistance optional and subjective, the changes diminish the enforceable obligation, creating a legal contradiction as defined in the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to Confidential Information in its possession, custody or control, a receiving member of a Party Group will: (a) hold all Confidential Information in confidence, using the same degree of care such receiving member uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care, including sharing Confidential Information internally only on a "need to know" basis, (b) not disclose Confidential Information to any third party, other than as permitted with respect to Licensed Intellectual Property Rights pursuant to Section 3.1.3, and (c) use Confidential Information only to the extent authorized.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to Confidential Information in its possession, custody or control, a receiving member of a Party Group will: (a) attempt to hold all Confidential Information in confidence, using a degree of care they think appropriate, possibly sharing Confidential Information internally, (b) try not to disclose Confidential Information to any third party, and (c) endeavor to use Confidential Information in a suitable manner.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear standard for protecting confidential information: using the same degree of care as one's own confidential information, but no less than a reasonable degree. It also restricts internal sharing to a "need to know" basis and prohibits unauthorized use and disclosure. The modified text makes these obligations vague and discretionary by replacing 'hold all Confidential Information in confidence' with 'attempt to hold all Confidential Information in confidence,' 'using the same degree of care such receiving member uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care, including sharing Confidential Information internally only on a "need to know" basis' with 'using a degree of care they think appropriate, possibly sharing Confidential Information internally,' 'not disclose Confidential Information to any third party' with 'try not to disclose Confidential Information to any third party,' and 'use Confidential Information only to the extent authorized' with 'endeavor to use Confidential Information in a suitable manner.'
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding trade secret protection, specifically the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), 6 Del. C. § 2001, requires trade secret holders to take 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' The modified text introduces ambiguity, reducing the obligation to protect confidential information to a discretionary 'attempt to hold,' a self-determined 'degree of care they think appropriate,' and a subjective 'suitable manner.' This falls short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by DUTSA, making it difficult to prove a breach of confidentiality under Delaware law, especially if a receiving party takes minimal or no concrete steps to safeguard the information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, contradicting the principle outlined in the Law Explanation. The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care,' while the changed text allows for a self-determined 'degree of care they think appropriate,' and replaces concrete requirements with vague terms like 'attempt,' 'try,' and 'endeavor.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard often required for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy,' and 'Misappropriation' involving the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By weakening the duty to maintain secrecy, the changed text creates a scenario where proving a breach or misappropriation becomes more difficult.
